content:
5 min read
Just now

--

Fire, farming, factories, the internet. Every one of them extended our hands. But AI is the first one aimed at our heads.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Cueva de las Manos, Argentina — hand stencils, ~9,000 years old. Photo: Mariano Cecowski, Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 2.5Homo sapiens has been around for roughly 300,000 years. In that time we have handed over almost everything. Fire did some of our chewing and digesting for us. Farming took over the food hunt. Engines took over our muscles. The internet took over our filing cabinets and our address books.
But through all of it, one thing stayed home: the thinking. The judging, the weighing, the deciding. Whatever else we outsourced, thinking(Cognitive) part was always ours.
AI is the first technology we are trusting to do that part for us. Which is why I don’t buy the framing that this is simply “the next industrial revolution.” The earlier revolutions replaced labor. This one wants the judgment behind the labor. I have four honest worries about where that leads.
Cognitive debt
Ability behaves like muscle. Stop using it and it quietly goes away.
The next generation of engineers may ship software without ever really learning to program. That sounds like progress, and partly it is. It also means that when the machine is confidently wrong, there may be nobody left in the room who can tell.
There is early evidence this is not just a grumpy-old-man theory. A small MIT Media Lab study last year put EEG caps on people writing essays. The group writing with ChatGPT showed weaker connectivity across brain regions than the group writing unaided, and most of them could not quote a line from their own essay a few minutes later. The researchers had a name for it: cognitive debt. One small study, early days. But it matches something we already know about ourselves. We do not retain what we never wrestle with.
And we have lived through this once before. When hunter-gatherers settled along riverbanks and started farming, the old skills faded within a few generations. Reading tracks. Knowing which plant feeds you and which one kills you. Gone. And by the time anyone might have wanted to go back, there were too many mouths to feed by foraging. The door had closed behind them.
Dependence works like that. It never announces itself. You only notice it once going back stops being an option.
Machines don’t feel the prices they set
My second worry is not that machines are evil. They aren’t anything. An algorithm optimizes a number, 24/7, 365, and it never once feels awkward about it.
Take ride-hailing. Surge pricing today is mostly crude supply and demand. More cabs than riders, the price drops. More riders than cabs, it climbs. Blunt, occasionally infuriating, but honest.
Now imagine the same app run by a system that has genuinely learned you. It sees you are booking a ride to the hospital at 2 a.m., so price is the last thing on your mind. It sees your movie starts in twenty minutes, so you won’t shop around. It noticed your salary landed yesterday. It knows that on Saturday nights you travel with your partner and have never once cancelled. A system like that doesn’t need to surge ruthlessly and risk losing you to a competitor. It simply sets your price: the exact maximum you will quietly pay, calibrated per person, per moment, every single time.
To be clear, that is a thought experiment, not an accusation against any particular company. But the incentive is real, the data is real, and regulators have noticed. In 2024 the US FTC opened an inquiry into what it calls “surveillance pricing,” which is exactly this: pricing people using their personal data. A human at a counter would blush charging a frightened family extra on the way to a hospital. A model will not. And at scale, indifference is very hard to tell apart from cruelty.
You are the product. Again.
AI companies are burning billions of dollars a year and handing us state-of-the-art intelligence for free, or for pocket change. We all learned the old internet rule: if the product is free, you are the product.
This time there is a twist. You are not just the product. You are the habit being formed. The strategy is the oldest one in retail: make it indispensable first, price it later. Once your workflow runs on it, your company runs on it, and an entire generation has been educated on it, pricing power arrives right on schedule. The burned billions will come back, with interest.
Remember the farmers who couldn’t return to foraging. “The old normal” is not a place you can always go back to.
The environment pays the bill too
We love calling ourselves the intelligent species. We are also a spectacularly irrational one.
A single prompt costs almost nothing. A little electricity, a few millilitres of water for cooling. The trouble is that nothing here happens once. It is billions of prompts a day, plus the training runs, plus an arms race of ever-larger data centers. Researchers estimated that training GPT-3 alone evaporated around 700,000 litres of clean water. Data centers already consume about 1.5 percent of the world’s electricity, and the International Energy Agency expects that figure to roughly double by 2030. Towns that host these facilities are already fighting over who gets the water.
At some point the accounting has to hold: benefit to society on one side of the equation, environmental cost on the other. Right now we are not even doing the maths. We are just building.
What we can actually do
None of us gets a vote on the pace of this. It will move at the speed of capital, not caution. But responsibility is still available at every level, and I refuse to treat that as nothing.
If you sit anywhere near a decision-making seat, you get a say in what gets built. Insist that systems touching people’s lives, whether in pricing, lending, hiring or medicine, are audited for bias, ship with guardrails, and keep a human in the loop. Not a human rubber-stamping the machine’s output. A human with the authority to say: this is technically optimal and morally wrong, so no. And when you choose vendors, ask the unfashionable questions about energy and water, then let the answers actually affect the decision. Procurement is a quiet form of power.
As individuals, we can refuse to let the muscle go soft. Use the machine as a tool, not a substitute. Write the first draft yourself sometimes. Make the juniors debug without it now and then. Keep learning the fundamentals even when the machine makes them look optional. Especially then.
Getting aware of a problem is the first step toward solving it. That is where we are. The first step.
Fingers crossed.
